_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Rock County, WI. The southeastern anchor of the Driftless cluster, Rock County sits at the glacial boundary — eastern townships are flat glacial outwash, western townships are Driftless hills. Janesville and Beloit developed as manufacturing centers on the Rock River, and the county's agricultural prosperity attracted both Yankee settlers and Norwegian immigrants.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Transitional zone: eastern Rock County is glacial outwash plain (flat, gravelly, well-drained); western Rock County is Driftless Area with unglaciated ridges and valleys. The glacial boundary runs north-south through the county's center. The Rock River cuts through both zones.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 750–1,100 ft. Janesville sits at approximately 800 ft on the Rock River. The Driftless hills in the western townships reach 1,100 ft.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Flat to gently rolling glacial terrain east of the Rock River; increasingly dissected Driftless hills west of the river. The Rock River corridor provided waterpower for early mills and later for manufacturing. The contrast between the flat eastern prairie (easy to plow, quick to settle) and the hilly western Driftless (harder to farm, settled later) shaped the county's development pattern.

soil_types.description: Deep, well-drained prairie soils on the glacial outwash plain. Among the most productive soils in southern Wisconsin.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Outstanding for wheat, then corn and dairy. Eastern Rock County farms were highly productive and attracted Yankee farmers seeking the best land available.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Rock County, Wisconsin. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1975.

soil_types.description: Shallow silt loam over dolomite bedrock on the Driftless ridgetops of western Rock County. Well-drained but thin.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Less productive than the glacial-plain soils. Farms on Driftless ridgetops were smaller and depended more on livestock than grain. Rock outcrops limited tillable acreage.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Rock County, Wisconsin, 1975.

major_waterways.name: Rock River
major_waterways.role: Primary drainage, industrial waterpower, settlement corridor
major_waterways.description: The Rock River enters from the north and flows south through Janesville and Beloit before crossing into Illinois. The river provided waterpower for early mills and factories and defined the county's urban corridor. Janesville's industries — woolen mills, agricultural implements, and later automobile manufacturing (GM plant, 1919) — sat on the Rock River.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Rock River gave Janesville its reason to exist — waterpower first, factory sites later — and by {year}, {ancestor_name} likely knew the river as the thing that ran behind the mill or the factory, not as the wild water the Ho-Chunk had fished.

major_waterways.name: Sugar River
major_waterways.role: Western county drainage, connection to Green County
major_waterways.description: Flows through the Driftless hills in the western part of the county before joining the Pecatonica. The Sugar River valley connected Rock County to the Swiss and Norwegian settlements of Green County.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Sugar River ran through the Driftless hills where the Norwegian farms sat on ridgetops that looked out over a landscape the glaciers had never touched.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Tallgrass prairie on the eastern glacial plain; oak openings; oak-hickory forest on Driftless slopes
vegetation_eras.description: The eastern half of Rock County was open prairie — big bluestem, Indian grass, and compass plant stretching to the horizon, maintained by fire. The Driftless west was forested with white oak, red oak, shagbark hickory, and black walnut on the ridges. Prairie occupied the valley floors and south-facing slopes.
vegetation_eras.source: Curtis, John T. The Vegetation of Wisconsin. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1959.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Wheat fields replacing prairie; forest cleared on Driftless ridgetops; prairie sod broken with steel plows
vegetation_eras.description: The prairie was broken for wheat with remarkable speed. John Deere's steel plow (manufactured just across the IL border) made prairie-breaking feasible. By 1860, most of the eastern county was under cultivation.
vegetation_eras.source: Nesbit, Robert C. Wisconsin: A History. 2nd ed. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Dairy farm mosaic: hay, corn, oats, pasture; urban and industrial expansion along Rock River
vegetation_eras.description: Dairy farming replaced wheat. The visual landscape became the familiar Wisconsin pattern: red barn, silo, pasture, hay field, and woodlot. Janesville and Beloit grew as manufacturing centers, consuming surrounding farmland.
vegetation_eras.source: Apps, Jerry. Wisconsin Agriculture: A History. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2015.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: The prairie east of Janesville in April 1845 was a sea of last year's dead grass, waist-high and tawny, broken by the black scars of recently burned patches where new green shoots pushed through the ash. A Yankee settler from Vermont or New York, accustomed to forests, stood in a landscape without a tree in sight for a mile in every direction. The wind was constant — a prairie wind that smelled of wet earth and old grass and carried the calls of prairie chickens booming on their display grounds. The sky was enormous. Underfoot, the sod was a dense mat of roots that no wooden plow could break — it would take John Deere's steel blade to turn this grass into wheat land. The silence was not silence; it was wind and birdsong and the creak of the covered wagon, but it had no walls, no echo, nothing to stop it from running to the horizon.
sensory_snapshots.source: Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989). Apps, Wisconsin Agriculture (2015).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: On a July morning at five o'clock, a Rock County dairy farmer was already in the barn — the cows waiting in their stanchions, the clank of milk pails, the warm animal smell of hay and manure and fresh milk. The barn was dark except for a kerosene lantern hung on a nail. Outside, the hay fields were silver with dew and the corn was shoulder-high in the bottom fields along the Rock River. By seven the milk was in the cans on the platform by the road, waiting for the cheese factory wagon. The heat was building — eighty-five by noon, humid, with thunderheads piling up in the west. The mosquitoes from the river bottoms were fierce. By evening the barn demanded the same routine — milking, feeding, cleaning — because the cows did not recognize Sundays, holidays, or exhaustion.
sensory_snapshots.source: Apps, Jerry. Every Farm Tells a Story. Stillwater, MN: Voyageur Press, 2005.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Janesville in January 1935 was a factory town holding its breath. The Rock River was frozen solid enough to walk across. The Parker Pen Company and the GM assembly plant — the two employers that kept Janesville alive — were running reduced shifts. Snow covered everything, smoothing the industrial edges of the riverfront into white mounds. The air smelled of coal smoke from every chimney in town and the sharp mineral cold that comes at ten below zero. Workers stamped snow off their boots at the factory gate and thanked God they still had a shift, because the farms outside town were worse — milk prices so low that dairymen poured milk on the ground in protest, and the co-op creamery checks bounced.
sensory_snapshots.source: Goldstein, Amy. Janesville: An American Story. New York: Simon & Schuster, 2017. Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989).

landscape_changes.description: Rock County was part of the first wave of Wisconsin settlement. Yankee migrants from New York, Vermont, and Connecticut arrived via the Great Lakes and the Rock River, claiming the flat prairie lands east of the river first. The steel plow broke the prairie sod, and wheat fields replaced tallgrass within a decade. Janesville was platted in 1836 and grew quickly as a river town.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Rock County in {year}, the prairie was disappearing under the plow faster than anyone could have imagined — a landscape that had taken ten thousand years to build turned into wheat fields in ten.

landscape_changes.change: General Motors assembly plant opens in Janesville
landscape_changes.description: The GM plant brought industrial employment at a scale Janesville had never seen. The plant assembled Chevrolets, Pontiacs, and later trucks, and became the economic anchor of the county for eighty-nine years. The plant's presence attracted parts suppliers and service industries, making Rock County an industrial economy alongside its dairy farms.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: When the GM plant opened in Janesville, {ancestor_name}'s family had a new option — factory wages, steadier than milk prices, paid every Friday instead of once a month from the creamery.
landscape_changes.source: Goldstein, Amy. Janesville: An American Story. New York: Simon & Schuster, 2017.

narrative_hooks: Rock County sat on the line between two Wisconsins — the flat glaciated east where the prairie had been and the steep Driftless west where the coulees ran deep. {ancestor_name}'s farm was on one side or the other, and the terrain told you which.

The Rock River gave Janesville its factories and {ancestor_name} a paycheck — waterpower first, then the GM plant, then the Parker Pen, all of them built along water that had once been Ho-Chunk fishing grounds.

When {ancestor_name} broke prairie sod east of Janesville, the grass roots went three feet deep and the steel plow screamed against them — but the soil underneath was black gold, and the wheat that grew in it paid for the farm.

In Rock County, the choice between farming and factory work was not a choice at all — many families did both, milking cows at five in the morning and working the GM line by seven, living in two economies simultaneously.
source_references: Nesbit, Robert C. Wisconsin: A History. 2nd ed. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.
Apps, Jerry. Wisconsin Agriculture: A History. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2015.
Goldstein, Amy. Janesville: An American Story. New York: Simon & Schuster, 2017.
Curtis, John T. The Vegetation of Wisconsin. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1959.
USDA Soil Survey of Rock County, Wisconsin. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1975.
